item.title: A little Magrittesque.
item.content:encoded: Fascinating, both on the psychological and philosophical aspect!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of like how pretentious that last note is. Exactly how an anartist would try to justify their work. I mean I agree with the above comments, it is a shark. They literally just put a shark in a public pool, the panic was justified and probably saved peoples lives by getting them out of the pool, but I love it shows how they thought they were being deep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sharkfuck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to punch it

item.title: Oh boy...
item.content:encoded: The Center is gonna have a time with this one.

item.title: A Shameful Displace: A Refutation of SCP-1057's Central Thesis
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1057 asks the reader to consider the possibility that the fear of sharks can be more dangerous than actual sharks. In real life, this is true: the mass-harvesting of sharks contributes to a sustained war on the oceanic ecosystem, with potentially dire consequences for the nutritional needs of several million humans alone. However, 1057 has no business asking such questions in the form of an aggressive tiger shark.

In the world of the Foundation, what is a shark? This isn't a question of biology, but a matter of platonics (see: SCP-609, an entry where such details matter): the trappings of a shark have meaning and application. Indeed, there are multiple ways a shark could be expressed, such as a biological entity with the physiological floorplan, as a large bitey fish-thing, or even as an outline. This is in addition to the consequences of a shark, be that the real-life behavior of real-life sharks, or the darkly-romanticized behavior of, say, Jaws.

In both cases, 1057 is a shark. It is outlined like a shark, with the organ system of a shark. Additionally, it acts like a tiger shark, plus or minus some aggression issues. By any sense of meaning, it is an expression of a shark with the consequences of a shark, an observable entity with tangible results.

In-universe, it is posited that 1057 highlights the dangers of panic, but this ignores the fact that there is reason to panic. 1057 is an aggressive shark introduced in a crowded and enclosed environment.

item.title: This is like Marmite
item.content:encoded: You either love it or hate it. For me, it's a +1.

item.title: Re: SCP-1057 Photo
item.content:encoded: I made it as light as I could in the new draft, but unfortunately making the shadow lighter than the sand isn't within my skill set without making the photo look severely edited/fake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent article, very good, but, even with all the explanation in the comments, there's one question that remains: How am I supposed to punch it?
+1

item.title: Re: SCP-1057 Photo
item.content:encoded: Better. If you could make the anti-shadow slightly lighter than the area around it, that'd be perfect.

item.title: Re: SCP-1057 Photo
item.content:encoded:
How's this? (It may need a few small touch ups for a few pixel areas, but here's the general idea.)

https://www.deviantart.com/themorbidmemories/art/SCP-1057-Edit-754694523?ga_submit_new=10%3A1531693437

item.title: Re: SCP-1057 Photo
item.content:encoded: Almost perfect. Do you think you could remove the other, smaller shark?

item.title: SCP-1057 Photo
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoy this SCP, but I think a photo would add to the file a lot! If you like the photo, I'd be honored if you would use mine. I tried to capture the visibility in salt water plus the anti-shadow under harsh light. If you'd like to use it, please just credit Morbid Memories (at the photo linked below) and the original file from Wikimedia.

https://www.deviantart.com/themorbidmemories/art/SCP-1057-726014273

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Tigershark3.jpg

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Feed it the dyes
Punch it until your arms break, then kick until your legs give out, then headbutt until you die.
SPC-1057 has been punched

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
The solution is obvious:
The SPC has a monk sit beside the tank and meditate on the concept of punching sharks.

How do you punch something you can't see??
How do you punch nothing??
This is an abomination!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's incredible that people still don't understand this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not invisible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Invisible Shark from AWCY. Not too original. Downvote.

item.title: +445th Upvote
item.content:encoded:
I stopped the 444 and the 100 discussions,

am I cool yet?

item.title: Re: So much expectation...
item.content:encoded:
You've misunderstood.

It's not the idea of a shark. It's never been the idea of a shark. The title isn't "the idea of a shark".

The idea of a shark is the panic in people's heads.

The SCP is the absence of a shark (an animate shark-shaped empty space — and it's not made of air, it's "made of" vacuum).

(No shark at all) killed two people. (Idea of a shark) killed five people.

And who says the Foundation doesn't care about why the fluorescent dyes work?

item.title: So much expectation...
item.content:encoded:
I finally read 1057 after reading references to it for many weeks. The Idea of a Shark is maybe one of the most influential SCPs around, and the most known of the AWCY.

Well, it was too much expectation. Sincerely, I expected something more… I don't know. Maybe it was precisely the building up that made it unavoidably lacking. It's just a shark made of air. It isn't the Idea of a shark. And the way it says that theories were created and disproved to explain why the fluorescent dye works, without any real example or further explanation… its a Foundation's document saying that we don't know and we don't care.

Not a downvote, because I respect its influence. But no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Imagine a big glass tank of water with a bright light shining down on it. Look at the bottom of the tank: there are ripples of light showing how the water is sloshing back and forth. If you put a stick halfway into the water, you can see how the different refractive indices of water and air cause the stick to appear as if it's broken.

Now, add a shark. A real one, so it's opaque. Observe how the light can't pass through the shark, with the result that there's a dark patch at the bottom of the tank. Beneath the shark, it's darker. There's a shadow.

Now, put a big canister of compressed air at the bottom… and open it. Huge air bubble forms at the bottom of the tank and quickly moves to the top. What do you see on the bottom of the tank, beneath the air bubble? Answer: the space directly beneath the air bubble is actually brighter than the space that's not beneath the air bubble (because air and water have different refractive indices). Not much brighter, but a detectable phenomenon. The bigger the air bubble, the larger the brightened space (or 'anti-shadow'). The anti-shadow doesn't last very long, normally, because the bubble rises up and leaves the water. If, on the other hand, the bubble were the exact size and shape of a live shark, and it stayed underwater?

That's not to say that SCP-1057 is an air bubble, though. It's not. The refractive index of air is 1.000277, and the refractive index of SCP-1057 is exactly 1 — which, incidentally, is the refractive index of vacuum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty cool! However, I do have a question about the "anti-shadow." What real-world property of air is this referring to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't need to be scary….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, you are supposed to not-punch the not-shark. This is why the SPC has so much trouble with this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, since it is -1 shark, it must be punched an appropriate negative number of times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I supposed to punch it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The title alone was enough to make me upvote. "Abscence of shark", that's just gold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but this is the executive overview. You want to know the exact temperature, the salinity, the other minerals? You go for document 1057-H-22 (as already mentioned). Here, all you need to know is "treated to replicate a temperate ocean environment".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think changing the sentence saying the tank is filled with ocean like water to salinity or put the salt level and saying its RO water with a salinaty of xxx. It would sound more professional and people who have salt water tanks would be more compelled to upvote for the effort put in to the details

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, you are cool now. +1
Cleverness means that the idea of a shark can get more upvotes than an actual shark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was confused by the initial comments on this until I realised it was a rewrite. Meaning no disrespect to the original author, comparing the two versions is a perfect illustration of both 'less is more' and the way that execution can make or break a concept.

I also disliked the AWCY reference (not enough to change my upvote) until I read the '-1 shark' explanation. This is one of those times where I wish we could upvote more than once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this. An imaginary shark, in the mathematical meaning of the word "imaginary", plus a really good "AWCY?" art installation.
Gets +1 vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The SPC's worst nightmare! Also helps that it got used in the excerpt from MC&D's catalog. So this skip managed to be of significance to three different GOI's? notbad.jpg
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forgive me for replying to an old comment, but there's no rating system for comments, and this one deserves praise. It is as smart and succinct as the article it explains. Beautiful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
HA! Creative.

+1 for you, and a +1 for the kids who are now cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this article. I also love AWCY?. This article is a perfect example of how to use them correctly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i really like this one. i really like how simple but fitting the containment is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When i was reading this, I thought it should be an AWCY item. And it was! Have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it killed two swimmers and mutilated a lifeguard; five people were killed in the ensuing panic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I no get it

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: As I understand the word contraindicated is usually used in context of medical treatment specifically pharmaceuticals.

I proudly kick this skip into this milestone!

This amuses me greatly. It's very artsy in this bizzare way. My favorite parts though are "Contraindicated" (excellent tone, and an underused word) and "suggested, tested, and disproved" (just tickles me. It gives this sense of "Fuck it! It's an absence of shark! That's as explained as we're getting!")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for this. I actually really like AWCY? and reading the explanations of their associated SCPs made me read more about art in general.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think FFB omitted a word or two, possibly by accident.

Try looking at it like this.

Panic means that the (idea of a shark) is more dangerous than (an actual shark). You got that part, right?

But what we have here is (the absence of a shark). An empty space in the shape of a shark. We could call that an "antishark". Or we could call it "no shark at all".

Panic — caused by people being afraid of a shark — turned out to be more dangerous than (no shark at all). The (no shark at all) killed two people and maimed a third — but, in their panic, the people who were fleeing, killed five among them.

The idea of a shark was more dangerous than an actual shark. It was even more dangerous than no shark at all.

This is the work of art. It clearly shows that humans, even humans who think that they have become reasonable and rational and civilized, can be more dangerous to themselves than an invisible apex predator that's dropped on them without warning. See how fragile civilization is? Even with lifeguards on duty, even with signs everywhere saying "NO RUNNING", even with plenty of safe places just outside the pool… they were unable to THINK. They just panicked. The absence of a shark killed two people and maimed a third, but the idea of a shark totally ripped through the minds of everyone on site. In the 70s, when the film Jaws came out, shark phobia was practically epidemic. People stopped going to beaches. People stopped going to pools. And when the sequel came out, the tag line was "just when you thought it was safe to go back in the water'.

The absence of a shark is anomalous and incomprehensible. It killed two people and mutilated a third.

The idea of a shark is totally normal and fully understood. It killed five people.

Do you understand now, ToxicClay? Are we cool yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so dig this.

Some things are, and some other things are not. Up until AWCY?, everyone and their mother, no matter how abstract, always made are about things that are with things that are in order to express points that are. AWCY? is a style of art known as "post phenomenological". Like Post-Modernism was a response to Modernism, Post-phenomenological (things that are not) art is a response to Phenomenological (things that are) art. AWCY? purposefully blurs the line between fiction and reality within the confines of their fictional universe. They're art for not-art's sake. They're not-art for art's sake.

…And with that, I probably muddied the waters even more. But it doesn't matter.

Are We Cool Yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
completely nonsensical and pointless until you read the little card with the artist's explanation, and then your perspective, your entire mind changes.

And if someone could please give me a card like that for AWCY, I would appreciate it, because I still don't get this:

the douchebag hipster who would join a pretentious, anomalous dadaist guerilla art movement that likes to namedrop itself just to make sure you know who just bazoopered you? Same guy who could let seven people die to teach a weird lesson about panic or something.

And maybe that's because I also don't get modern art, but AWCY makes no attempt to make itself understood. I understand the Foundation. The Insurgency. MC&D. I don't get AWCY, and based on conversations it seems there's just something I won't be able to comprehend, and until someone's able to break shit down Barney-style, it appears that will continue.

To address the specific concern about it being 'forced,' as my comment implied, this SCP stands fantastically on its own merits, and the tacked-on-feeling reference to AWCY feels off to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you.

I've actually been to modern art galleries. Of my own free will, no less. And more than once, I've seen exhibits that look completely nonsensical and pointless until you read the little card with the artist's explanation, and then your perspective, your entire mind changes.

That was what I was trying for with this. And when I added AWCY to the GOI page to bring them to greater attention, it's what I hoped people would use AWCY for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't AWCY? bullshit, in my opinion. This is AWCY? done correctly. Hell, it was an artistic statement, and I love that about this piece, because it actually said something coherent and understandable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not fucking supposed to be amusing. Frankly, I'm rather irked that so many people let a few less-than-quality SCPs that failed to execute the idea of AWCY? properly sour them completely on a genuinely interesting and high-potential concept just because those SCPs had the misfortune of being posted in the same general timeframe. That's literally the primary reason people are tired of it, because there are . And I think it was over a year ago. There are plenty of genuinely good AWCY? articles on the site, and I would wager that they're all rated lower than they really should be just because people can't get over the shit fits they threw then.

I'm not saying that there aren't grounds to dislike AWCY?, I'm saying that you're ignoring the reasons that exist to like their inclusion here:

Panic means that the idea of a shark can be more dangerous than an actual shark.

It can even be more dangerous than no shark at all.

Are we cool yet?

Read the note. Take a second, grab a glass of water to wash the bad taste of bad AWCY? out of your mouth, and actually read that last note. That third line completes the note. Each line is shorter, more unusual, and more open to interpretation than the last, which is fantastic structure. If you (or someone else) can think of a better way to cap off the piece, I would love to hear it.

While I think it stands perfectly fine in a vacuum, because I'm forced to acknowledge that the phrase 'are we cool yet?' has a meaning associated with it, here's an explanation of 'this AWCY bullshit': the douchebag hipster who would join a pretentious, anomalous dadaist guerilla art movement that likes to namedrop itself just to make sure you know who just bazoopered you? Same guy who could let seven people die to teach a weird lesson about panic or something. Which is why this is a freaking perfect fit for both the piece and the GoI. I'm not seeing the part where it's hackneyed and forced; perhaps you could spell it out for me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welp. I was all happy and prepared to upvote this for being a thoroughly amusing piece of work…

…and then AWCY happened. And my upvote burrowed into the earth's core, where heat and pressure changed it into a fiery, wrathful downvote.

Seriously. Can someone explain this AWCY bullshit to me? It's hackneyed, forced, and overall unamusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. Any inspiration taken from Hirst's The Physical Impossibility of Death in the Mind of Someone Living? (link)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Frankly, this earned my +1 just for the idea of a not-shark. Very nice touch of "the man who wasn't there", but with some real teeth to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is probably just my inner hipster talking, but I really like AWCY, just for the fact that they are so forced. They try so damn hard, and yet noone gets them. It's like looking at the mad scientist version of a starving artist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah, it refers to entering 1057's tank being forbidden.
I suspect the original author has a first language other than english, it's just an unusual formulation I myself am prone to fairly often.
